paragraphs.text: From the catalog we built, **Cu(II)** is especially data-rich: at least **20 highly populated Cu(II) ligand pairs** were surfaced immediately, including ammonia, imidazole, ethylenediamine, glycine, acetate, histidine, and hydroxide, with tens of measured stability entries per pair. In contrast, the current retrieved evidence for Fe systems is not yet populated in the same summary, so the strongest SRD-46-backed statement right now is that **copper—especially Cu(II)—has much broader stability-constant coverage than iron in the records surfaced so far**.

paragraphs.text: Chemistry insight: this broad Cu(II) coverage is consistent with copper’s rich aqueous coordination chemistry and the historical popularity of Cu(II) as a benchmark ion for amines, amino acids, and N-donor ligands. Iron is often more condition-sensitive—especially Fe(III) because of strong hydrolysis—so fair comparison usually requires tighter matching of pH, ionic strength, and ligand class.
